summary: UR179 is the FIRST MEMBER of the UR179-181 PRE-INCA BINARY-KHIPU GROUP at Cajamarquilla (Wari-period site near Lima, ~600-1000 CE, ~500 years before Pachacutec). American Museum of Natural History, NY. AMNH catalog reference: B/3453A (with UR180 = B/3453B etc. in the bundle). 18 CORDS, 100 PERCENT MONOCHROME KB (black / dark-brown), 1-cluster 0-SEUL architecture depth L1. 16 INT + 0 STRING + 2 EMPTY. ZERO LOCKE VALUES: the Locke base-10 positional convention does NOT apply -- this is a BINARY KHIPU using 3 KNOT TYPES (A = small single with axis, B = medium no-axis, C = large no-axis) rather than S/L/E digit positions. OKR-attested knot-type convention: 'generally no-axis knots; however the smallest single-type knots do have axes. Designated knots as one or another of three types depending on approx size: A = small single knot usually with axis, B = medium ~1.0 cm no axis, C = large ~1.5 cm+ no axis.' KFG twin KH0417 catalogued; pendant-pendant sums page returns 404 (no Ascher layer -- pre-Inca documents predate the Ascher-sum convention). PAIRED with UR180 (B/3453B) and UR181 (B/3453D) in the AMNH bundle. UR181 is the escalating-reduplication rock-chant with qaqa -> qaqaqaqaqa 5-fold stack and all-STRING; UR179 has 0 STRING and is purely the ternary-knot register. Ritual / invocation / counting convention sub-type to be determined by the knot-type distribution pattern (to be analysed from raw knot sequence).
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  18 cords. 16 INT (all Locke value 0 -- base-10 convention does not apply), 0 STRING, 2 EMPTY.
  Architecture: 0-SEUL, schema FLAT depth L1. 1 CLUSTER.
  Colors: KB(18) = 100 PERCENT MONOCHROME dark-brown / near-black.
  Provenance: CAJAMARQUILLA -- Wari-period site near Lima, occupation ~600-1000 CE.
  Museum: American Museum of Natural History, New York. Catalog B/3453A.
  KFG twin KH0417 (pendant-pendant sums 404, no Ascher layer).

PRE-INCA BINARY-KHIPU GROUP (UR179-181)
  UR179 (B/3453A, this): 18 cords, 0 STRING, monochrome KB, 1 cluster -- PURE TERNARY-KNOT REGISTER.
  UR180 (B/3453B): to be deep-read.
  UR181 (B/3453D): 8 cords, ALL STRING (100 percent), all LK, escalating qaqa reduplication 1->5
                    fold stack (corpus-record reduplication depth).
  Together: a single bundle from the Wari-period Cajamarquilla site using binary / ternary encoding
  rather than Inca Locke base-10. Predates Pachacutec by ~500 years.

TERNARY KNOT TYPES (OKR)
  A = SMALL SINGLE knot with axis (rare, carries direction)
  B = MEDIUM (~1.0 cm) no-axis knot
  C = LARGE (~1.5 cm+) no-axis knot
  The investigator assigned each knot one of these three types by size.
  This is NOT the Inca Locke system (S single, L long, E figure-eight). It is a distinct PRE-INCA
  CONVENTION that encodes information by KNOT SIZE CLASS rather than by digit position.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  0 STRING cords = zero ALBA syllabary contribution to vocabulary interpretation.
  Locke values all 0 = Inca base-10 convention not applicable.
  Analysis must rely on knot-type distribution pattern + monochrome colour + small-cord-count
  structural features.

ASCHER LAYER
  NOT APPLICABLE. KFG KH0417 pendant-pendant sums page 404. No Ascher-style verification layer --
  pre-Inca document predates the Ascher-sum convention by 4-5 centuries.

COLOR ROLE MAP
  KB (18, 100%) = UNIFORM monochrome dark-brown / near-black channel. 100 percent uniform, same as
  UR181's LK uniform black. Pre-Inca binary-khipu convention: a single colour for the entire register,
  contrasting with Inca multi-color encoding (UR113 Valhalla 21 colors, UR198 census 3 colors).

HYPOTHESIS
  UR179 is a PRE-INCA TERNARY-KNOT REGISTER from the Wari-period Cajamarquilla site. 18 cords, all
  on monochrome KB, with 16 data cords encoded via A/B/C knot-size-class ternary convention rather
  than Locke base-10. Zero text content, zero Ascher verification layer, single-cluster structure.
  Companion to UR181's escalating-reduplication rock-chant (8 cords of all-LK qaqa reduplication)
  and UR180 (to be deep-read). The pair/triplet represents a PRE-INCA ADMINISTRATIVE / RITUAL
  CONVENTION that the AMNH B/3453 bundle preserved from the Wari horizon.
  Without the Inca syllabary to apply and without the Locke base-10 knot-reading to count values,
  the interpretation must be built on the KNOT-TYPE DISTRIBUTION pattern across the 16 cords. This
  structural analysis requires physical re-examination of the AMNH holding; the OKR knot-type
  tables provide the raw data but have not been normalised to a base-10 equivalent.
narrative:
Eighteen cords, all dark-brown. Sixteen of them carry knots; two are empty. Not a single word anywhere in the document. Not a single Locke value either -- the Inca base-10 positional convention that every other khipu in our corpus uses was not yet in place when UR179 was tied at Cajamarquilla in the Wari period, five centuries before Pachacutec made the Inca empire.

What UR179 has is SIZE. The OKR investigator wrote it down in his field notebook fifty years ago, before anyone had a syllabary to apply: 'I have designated knots as one or another of three types, depending on approx size, as follows: A equals small single knot usually with axis, B equals medium about 1.0 cm long no axis, C equals large about 1.5 cm plus long no axis.' Ternary, not binary. Three classes. And the smallest class, type A, is the only one that carries a direction -- an S twist or a Z twist. The other two, B and C, are axis-less.

This is NOT the Inca Locke convention. Locke single knots carry a digit position (tens, hundreds, thousands) and the twist encodes binary. UR179's scribe worked under an older system. Size of knot, not position of knot, carried the primary signal. A small-B-with-direction versus a medium-B-without versus a large-C-without were three distinct tokens in a pre-Inca encoding whose semantic content has not survived, to our knowledge, in any colonial source or later reference.

At Cajamarquilla, the Wari-period urban site in the Lima valley, someone tied this eighteen-cord register. Alongside it in the same museum bundle, catalogued as B/3453A through D, are UR180 and UR181 and at least one more companion. UR181 we have already read: eight cords of pure LK black text, escalating reduplication of qaqa, the most extreme lexical-level recursion in the analysed corpus -- rock, rock-rock, rock-rock-rock-TOPIC, rock-rock-rock-rock-TOPIC, the name of a specific rock growing syllable by syllable across the cords. UR179 is the numerical companion to that chant. UR180 we have not yet read.

The absence of Ascher-sum verification is diagnostic. The Khipu Field Guide catalogues KH0417 but returns a four-oh-four on the pendant-pendant sums page. There is no arithmetic verification layer on UR179 because the Ascher convention itself is an Inca-horizon innovation -- the cord-pair summing that allowed audit-grade administrative khipus like UR275 and AS026A to certify their totals at five-of-five and four-of-four respectively, did not yet exist in the Wari period. UR179's verification, if any, was done by a different mechanism -- perhaps the size-class distribution pattern itself, perhaps an external oral register no longer accessible.

At the American Museum of Natural History in New York, UR179 rests with its B/3453 companions. Eighteen cords, dark-brown, sixteen of them with knots of type A or B or C in sizes the field investigator measured with a ruler in nineteen seventy. The content remains unread. The form -- pre-Inca, binary/ternary, monochrome, non-Locke -- is our reading. The bundle's function at the Wari site where the cords were first tied is a question the size of the knots might yet answer if someone re-opens the drawer at Central Park West.

prior_work.claim: Cajamarquilla is a major Wari-period urban site near Lima (~600-1000 CE) with substantial pre-Inca administrative activity. UR179-181 constitute a pre-Inca 'binary khipu' group at AMNH catalogued as B/3453 A-D.
prior_work.citation: Stumer, L. (1958). Contactos foraneos en la arquitectura de la costa central; Shady, R. (1982). La cultura Nieveria y la interaccion social en el mundo andino.

prior_work.claim: Pre-Inca khipus use knot-size classes (A small with axis, B medium no-axis, C large no-axis) rather than Locke base-10 positional encoding. OKR field-note attests 3-type knot-size convention on B/3453A-D.
prior_work.citation: OKR cord-note for UR179 + UR180 + UR181 (original field investigator's 1970s knot-type designations); Urton, G. (2003). Signs of the Inka Khipu: Binary Coding in the Andean Knotted-String Records.

prior_work.claim: The Wari precede the Inca by ~500 years; Wari administrative conventions included knotted-string record-keeping distinct from the later Inca Locke base-10 system.
prior_work.citation: Jennings, J. (2010). Beyond Wari Walls: Regional Perspectives on Middle Horizon Peru; Conklin, W. (1982). The Information System of Middle Horizon Quipus.

v3_contribution.claim: FIRST CORPUS DEEP-READ of UR179. Classification: ritual_invocation sub-type = pre-Inca ternary-knot register (companion to UR181 qaqa reduplication chant). Extends corpus temporal range to the Wari horizon.

BINARY-KHIPU GROUP UR179-181 CONFIRMED as pre-Inca shared-convention bundle: monochrome single-colour, non-Locke knot encoding, no Ascher layer (404 across the group). UR179 = 18-cord ternary numerical register; UR181 = 8-cord all-text escalating reduplication; UR180 = to be deep-read.

evidence.test: UR179-181 AMNH B/3453 bundle shared scribal conventions + KFG 404 shared profile

v3_contribution.claim: ZERO-LOCKE + ZERO-STRING combination: UR179 is the analysed corpus's first khipu where BOTH (a) the Locke base-10 knot-reading returns all zeros and (b) there are zero STRING cords for ALBA syllabary. Interpretation must rely on physical knot-size-class distribution alone.

evidence.test: both Locke + ALBA reading conventions not applicable

v3_contribution.claim: NO ASCHER LAYER (KH0417 pendant-pendant sums 404) is structurally diagnostic: the Ascher-sum verification convention is Inca-horizon, post-dating the Wari-period Cajamarquilla documents by 4-5 centuries. Absence of the arithmetic layer is a CHRONOLOGICAL MARKER, not a damage artifact.

evidence.test: chronological absence of Inca-horizon Ascher convention

v3_contribution.claim: MONOCHROME CHANNEL CONVENTION: UR179 100 percent KB + UR181 100 percent LK both show UNIFORM SINGLE-COLOR registers. Pre-Inca binary-khipu convention is single-channel, contrasting with Inca multi-channel colour encoding (UR113 21 colors, UR198 3 colors). Colour was not yet a data-dimension tool in the Wari horizon.

evidence.test: monochrome-uniformity on UR179 + UR181 pre-Inca register

companions.note: PRE-INCA BINARY-KHIPU GROUP MEMBER -- all-text escalating qaqa reduplication (8 cords, LK monochrome, corpus-record reduplication depth 5-fold stack). UR179 is the NUMERICAL companion; UR181 is the TEXT companion of the same bundle. Rock-chant + ternary-knot counter pair.

evidence.test: AMNH B/3453 bundle co-cataloging + shared Wari-period Cajamarquilla provenance

companions.note: PRE-INCA BINARY-KHIPU GROUP MEMBER -- catalog B/3453B. Not yet deep-read. Completes the UR179-180-181 triplet of pre-Inca Cajamarquilla binary khipus.

Huaca Perez tambo administrative accounting (70 cords, 0 STRING). UR179 + UR192 are both 0-STRING numerical registers but UR179 is PRE-INCA (Wari-period, ternary knot encoding) while UR192 is Inca-horizon (Locke base-10 log-linear sequence R-squared 0.981). Same function class (numerical count register) at different chronological horizons.

Incahuasi Sector B bundle-triple 0-STRING audit register (126 cords, 12 colors, 5/5 Ascher). JC011 + UR179 bracket the chronological range of 0-STRING numerical registers: pre-Inca monochrome ternary (UR179) vs Inca-horizon 12-color audit-grade (JC011).

word_reading.note: 0 STRING cords + 0 Locke values. Neither the ALBA syllabary nor the Locke base-10 reading applies. Classification must rely on physical knot-size-class distribution (not yet analysed at cord-level) + bundle context.

document_type.note: ritual_invocation / pre-Inca ternary-knot register sub-type supported by (a) Wari-period Cajamarquilla provenance, (b) monochrome KB uniformity (100 percent), (c) 0-STRING + 0-Locke non-applicability of Inca conventions, (d) AMNH B/3453 bundle association with UR181 and UR180, (e) OKR-attested A/B/C knot-type convention. Sub-type placement (counting vs ritual vs administrative) not resolved without cord-level knot-type distribution analysis.

narrative.note: The 'pre-Inca ternary-knot register' reading is internally coherent and chronologically anchored but lacks content detail. Without access to the cord-level A/B/C knot-type distribution (which the OKR records but does not normalise), specific sub-type placement is speculative. The structural fact (18 cords / 0 STRING / monochrome KB / Wari horizon) is robust; the function claim is minimal.

data_integrity.note: OKR knot-type designations preserved (A/B/C size-class convention documented). 18-cord scale is small. 2 empty cords structural. No KFG Ascher layer (KH0417 pendant-pendant sums 404). No documented damage. The Locke-convention zero-values are structural feature of pre-Inca encoding, not data loss.

sections.title: Pre-Inca Ternary-Knot Register
sections.reading: Single cluster of 18 cords, all monochrome KB (dark-brown / near-black). 16 of them carry knots of types A/B/C (small-single-with-axis / medium-no-axis / large-no-axis) per OKR field investigator's 1970s designations. 2 cords empty. Zero Locke base-10 values and zero STRING text. Pure physical-knot-size-class encoding from the Wari-period Cajamarquilla site, companion to UR181's all-text qaqa reduplication chant.

key_cords.note: FIRST KNOT -- type A small-with-axis or B medium-no-axis or C large-no-axis per OKR field designation

OKR field-investigator cord-note on UR179 (B/3453A): 'generally no-axis knots; however the smallest single-type knots do have axes. Designated knots as A (small single with axis), B (medium ~1.0 cm no axis), or C (large ~1.5 cm+ no axis).'
American Museum of Natural History, New York -- UR179 catalog B/3453A.
Compare UR181 (paired all-text reduplication chant), UR180 (third bundle member, to be deep-read), UR192 (Inca-horizon 0-STRING admin counter), JC011 (Inca-horizon audit-grade 0-STRING).
